view.slice.about: The 2D slice is a map of Mars seen from above. It takes one specific atmospheric quantity, for example temperature, pressure, the amount of dust or the strength of the wind, and shows its value everywhere on the planet using a colour gradient. The map corresponds to a chosen altitude (from the ground up to the high atmosphere) and to a given local time, a bit like a photo taken at a precise moment of the Martian day. At a single glance you can spot the big contrasts: the icy poles against the warmer equator, the effect of the terrain such as the huge Hellas basin or the giant volcanoes of Tharsis, or the gap between the sunlit side and the side plunged into night. It is often the first view people look at, because it shows straight away where something is happening, before studying it more closely with the other tools.
view.animation.about: The animation shows a full day on Mars, what scientists call a "sol". It strings together 48 maps taken one after another over the hours, always at the same altitude, and plays them like a short film. This way you can watch the atmosphere move instead of freezing it in a single image. Because the Martian air is very thin, far less dense than on Earth, it warms up and cools down very quickly as the Sun rises and then sets: between the afternoon and the night, the temperature can drop by several tens of degrees. Watching the film, you can see this wave of heat travel around the planet at the rhythm of the day, with the dust and the thin ice clouds following that same daily motion. This change over time, impossible to guess from a still map, is essential to understand how the Martian climate really works.
view.timeseries.about: The time series follows a single quantity at one precise point on Mars, identified by its latitude and longitude, and shows how it changes hour by hour over the course of a Martian day. Instead of looking at the whole planet at once, you focus on one place, for example a rover landing site, to observe its daily rhythm. The curve reveals things like the afternoon warming, the night-time cooling, or the hours when the wind blows hardest. It is very handy for comparing one location with another, or for preparing a mission that has to operate at certain times of day. At a glance you can read off at what hour a phenomenon reaches its maximum or its minimum.
view.profile.about: The vertical profile shows how a quantity changes with altitude, at a given place and time. You start from the ground and climb through the atmosphere: the curve shows, for example, how the temperature falls, or sometimes rises again, as you go higher. On Mars as on Earth, the air does not have the same properties low down and high up, and it splits into layers that behave differently. This graph is essential to understand the structure of the atmosphere, to spot the places where the temperature inverts, or to find out how high the dust and the clouds reach. It is the go-to tool for exploring the vertical dimension, the one that maps seen from above cannot show.
view.crosssection.about: The vertical cross-section slices the atmosphere the way you would cut a cake, to see its inside over the full height. Depending on the setting you choose, it shows the quantity as a function of altitude and latitude (from one pole to the other), or as a function of altitude and longitude (going around the globe). This gives you a picture of the atmosphere in depth, and no longer only at the surface. It lets you see how the temperature or the winds are organised with height from one region to another, for example the contrast between the equator and the poles through the whole thickness of the air. It is the ideal view for linking what happens at the ground with what happens much higher up.
view.hovmoller.about: The Hovmöller diagram brings space and time together in a single image. One axis represents position (latitude or longitude), the other represents the hours of the Martian day, and the colour gives the value of the quantity. On a single graph you can then follow how a pattern moves or repeats over time, for example a heat wave that advances or a phenomenon that comes back every day at the same hour. It is a tool much loved by climatologists, because it reveals the rhythms and the movements that you cannot see on a frozen map. It takes a little getting used to in order to read it, but it packs a huge amount of information into one image.
view.zonalmean.about: The zonal mean averages a quantity all the way around the planet, for each latitude, and then displays it as a function of altitude. By erasing the local details, it brings out the overall structure of the atmosphere, from one pole to the other and from the ground up to the top. You can clearly see the broad trends, such as the drop in temperature towards the poles or the stacking of air layers with altitude. It is a representation widely used in climate science, because it sums up at once the average behaviour of the whole planet. It complements the 2D map nicely: one shows the details, the other the big picture.
view.windrose.about: The wind rose sums up, at a given point, where the wind comes from and how strong it is, throughout the Martian day. Each branch points towards a direction, and the longer and more colourful it is, the more often and the harder the wind blows that way. This makes the dominant directions immediately obvious, for example a wind coming mostly from the west, or one that turns depending on the hour. This tool, borrowed from weather forecasting on Earth, is valuable for studying the circulation of the air and for preparing missions, because the wind influences landing, the dust that gets lifted and future energy sources such as wind turbines. At a glance, you grasp how windy a place is.
view.difference.about: This view compares two datasets by subtracting one from the other, then displays the result on a map. The colours show where the quantity has increased, where it has decreased, and by how much, compared with the reference situation. It is used, for example, to compare two seasons, two Martian years, or the same region before and after an event such as a dust storm. Rather than looking at two maps side by side, you see the change directly, which makes even the small differences stand out. It is the perfect tool for answering the question: what has changed, and where?
view.temporalprofile.about: The temporal profile brings altitude and time together in a single image, above one precise point on Mars. One axis gives the height in the atmosphere, the other the hours of the day, and the colour the value of the quantity. This way you can see how the whole column of air, from the ground up to the top, evolves over the course of the day, for example heat that gradually reaches the upper layers in the afternoon before dissipating at night. It is a rich way of looking at the same time at the vertical structure and its daily rhythm, two things that are usually studied separately. Ideal for analysing in detail the breathing of the atmosphere above one place.

about.title: About
about.intro: Mars Climate Viewer opens up the atmospheric data of the planet Mars, produced by the GEM-Mars climate model. The idea is to explore that data straight in the browser, with nothing to install, whether you are a researcher, a student or simply curious.
about.institute: Royal Belgian Institute for Space Aeronomy (BIRA-IASB)
about.dataCredit: Data from the GEM-Mars model, developed at the Royal Belgian Institute for Space Aeronomy.
